input.description: Show current state of orphan-cleanup click

input.command: nsg click amend c-momm4abc --goal "Failed-trial orphan cleanup — when guild-setup fails after codex-setup succeeds, the rig fails without running teardowns, leaving codexes registered in the lab-host's Scriptorium and bare repos on disk. With phase-2 daemon-fixture work landed, the same orphan pattern now also applies to test-guild daemon processes: a setup that succeeds + a downstream failure leaves a detached \`nsg start\` process running with its pidfile at <testGuild>/.nexus/daemon.pid, surviving across lab-host runs. Two design directions for the cleanup: (1) setup engines grab a release-on-rig-terminal handle that fires on any rig terminal-state, OR (2) teardowns run unconditionally on rig failure (currently they only run when the upstream chain succeeded). Whichever direction wins, the eventual recovery scan must also SIGTERM lingering daemon pids of failed/completed trials. Surfaced during live verifies. Cosmetic for now (orphans don't run code maliciously) but failed-trial dev loops accumulate cruft and orphan daemons can hold ports open." 2>&1 | tail -10
input.description: Amend orphan-cleanup click to include daemon pidfiles

commissionable: false

- session: 927ab84f-c92d-4a65-94ac-a19daf97ee42
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "Trial 1 take 2 surfaced a second bug — archive engine's 'missing required given writ' fired because the archive-phase orchestrator's graft didn't pass the writ through. Sean correctly flagged this — Spider's resolveGivens (plugins/spider/src/spider.ts:2010) DOES substitute \${writ} for graft givens at spawn time, same as for the static template's pre-graft engines. So the fix isn't a Spider bug or a clerk-fetch workaround — it's just adding 'writ: \"\${writ}\"' to buildArchiveGraft's emitted givens, matching the static template's pattern. Reverted the brief clerk-fetch detour and updated tests + docstring to reflect the proper mechanism. Added a regression test for author-givens-override-default precedence. 210/210 passing."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: 927ab84f-c92d-4a65-94ac-a19daf97ee42
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "🎉 Laboratory apparatus's first successful real-world trial completed end-to-end. X016 phase 1 — apparatus validation. Trial w-mondjwk9-5f18bff39e29, rig rig-mondjzr3-3c449c19, archive lar-mondkc1h-ed27ba9f0587, ~30s wallclock. All 14 engines completed in 1 attempt: 5 phase orchestrators + 2 fixtures (codex + guild) + 1 scenario + 3 probes + 1 archive + 2 teardowns. Validates the full pipeline: vibers-as-lab-host orchestrating, npx -p @shardworks/nexus@0.1.292 nsg init bootstrapping a fresh test guild, per-plugin install loop via test guild's local nsg (stacks/tools/codexes/clerk @ 0.1.292), trial codex registered into test guild via duck-typed upstream discovery, lab.commission-post-xguild posting the X016 brief into test guild's clerk via the test guild's local nsg, three probes capturing real data (probe-trial-context with lab-host context + manifest snapshot, probe-stacks-dump opening test guild's nexus.db read-only and capturing 1 writ in clerk/writs + 0 in clerk/links, probe-git-range walking the codex bare and confirming 0 commits as expected), archive engine writing one row to lab-trial-archives, both teardowns running successfully (no orphan fixtures left). Extracted artifacts to experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/artifacts/2026-05-01-baseline-validation/ — manifest.yaml + README.md (top-level archive metadata) + trial-context.yaml + stacks-export/clerk-writs.json (the real captured writ row, brief body intact, codex pointing at the disposable bare repo)."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: 927ab84f-c92d-4a65-94ac-a19daf97ee42
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "Renamed probe-trial-context fields to disambiguate lab-host vs test-guild scopes. `frameworkVersion` → `labHostFrameworkVersion`, `pluginsInstalled` → `labHostPluginsInstalled`. The trial-pinned framework version is in `manifestSnapshot.frameworkVersion` (separate from lab-host orchestration version). Caught in X016 trial 1 read-through — Sean noted the top-level `frameworkVersion: 0.0.0` in trial-context.yaml looked wrong (vibers' dev-source value vs the trial-pinned 0.1.292 in manifestSnapshot). Two follow-up clicks filed: c-monew2rg (X016 phase 2 — daemon lifecycle in lab.guild-setup, prerequisite for actual orientation-tax measurement) and c-monewa82 (rigTemplate null — investigate why Spider's RigDoc doesn't expose template name where the probe expects). 210/210 tests passing."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: 9a768036-061d-404d-b306-b3a72c49d5f4
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "Laboratory phase 2 — daemon lifecycle. Added new fixture pair `lab.daemon-setup` / `lab.daemon-teardown` (separate from guild-setup so daemon lifecycle is opt-in per trial; matches existing fixture decomposition pattern). Setup auto-allocates ephemeral 127.0.0.1 ports for the tool HTTP server and Oculus, deep-merges them into the test guild's guild.json, refuses if a stale `.nexus/daemon.pid` exists, then shells out `<localNsg> --guild-root <X> start` — `nsg start` already does its own detach (re-execs --foreground with detached:true + unref, stdio piped to .nexus/logs/daemon.{out,err}) and its own startup-sync (10s deadline polling pidfile + tool-server reachability), so the engine is a thin wrapper. Teardown shells out `nsg stop` (idempotent SIGTERM→SIGKILL with pidfile cleanup). Reverse-topo teardown order is daemon→guild→codex; daemon stops before guild-teardown rm-rf's the dir. Per-trial port allocation avoids collisions with the lab-host's daemon (defaults 7470/7471) and with concurrent trials. 18 new tests (228/228 total passing); typecheck clean; build clean. README updated with a new `Driving rigs with a test-guild daemon` section showing the four-fixture wiring pattern. c-momm4abc orphan-cleanup click amended to note daemon pidfiles are part of the orphan inventory."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=Coco GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=coco@nexus.local git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
laboratory: lab.daemon-setup/teardown fixture pair for phase-2 trials

Phase 2 trials want the test guild to actually execute the rig:
the scenario engine posts a commission, then waits for the resulting
writ to reach a terminal classification. That requires a running
guild daemon inside the test guild — Spider's crawl loop, Clockworks
dispatch, and the tool HTTP server all need to be running.

`nsg start` already does the heavy lifting: built-in detach, pidfile,
log piping, 10s startup-sync polling tool-server reachability,
idempotent. `nsg stop` is the symmetric companion (SIGTERM→SIGKILL
with pidfile cleanup, idempotent). So the daemon-fixture engines
are thin wrappers.

Shape: separate fixture pair rather than steps appended to
lab.guild-setup. Daemon lifecycle is opt-in per trial (X016 phase 1
doesn't need it; spinning a daemon costs ~10s + adds orphan-cleanup
risk), matches existing fixture decomposition (codex separate from
guild), and composes correctly with future patterns (multi-daemon
trials, daemon-restart-mid-trial, etc.).

Setup flow: validate optional toolServerPort/oculusPort givens
(integer in [1, 65535]); discover the test guild from upstream
yields (duck-type {guildName, guildPath}); auto-allocate ephemeral
127.0.0.1 ports via net.createServer().listen(0) + close when not
provided; deep-merge {tools.serverPort, oculus.port} into the test
guild's guild.json (preserving everything guild-setup wrote);
refuse if .nexus/daemon.pid exists (catches stale-state from a
prior failed teardown); shell out `<localNsg> start` (returns once
tool server is reachable); read pidfile for the yield. Per-trial
port allocation avoids collisions with the lab-host's daemon
(defaults 7470/7471).

Teardown flow: discover test guild from upstream; archive-presence
safety check (consistent with guild-teardown / codex-teardown);
shell out `<localNsg> stop` (tolerant — idempotent and skipped
when the local nsg binstub is missing). Does NOT delete files —
guild-teardown owns the rm-rf.

Reverse-topo teardown order with the canonical fixture wiring is
daemon→guild→codex: daemon stops first; then guild dir is rm'd
(taking the .nexus/daemon.pid with it); then codex bare repo is
cleaned.

18 new tests; 228/228 total passing; typecheck and build clean.
README updated with a `Driving rigs with a test-guild daemon`
section showing the four-fixture wiring pattern and the daemon
fixture's behavior. c-momm4abc orphan-cleanup click amended to
note daemon pidfiles are part of the orphan inventory.

Session: 9a768036-061d-404d-b306-b3a72c49d5f4
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -10

content.text:
Engines + tests + README + log committed. Let me take stock.

## Status

**Landed in this thread:**
- `lab.daemon-setup` / `lab.daemon-teardown` engines (`packages/laboratory/src/engines/daemon-fixture.ts`)
- 18 new unit tests covering port allocation, port-config deep-merge, upstream test-guild discovery, validation branches, the pidfile pre-flight guard, the archive-row safety check on teardown, and the missing-binstub tolerance — 228/228 passing
- README updated with a `Driving rigs with a test-guild daemon` section showing the four-fixture wiring pattern
- `c-momm4abc` amended to flag daemon pidfiles as part of the orphan inventory

The `c-monew2rg` click is still live — engines exist but no phase-2 trial has actually run against them yet, so we haven't validated the real-world flow.

## What's next

Three reasonable directions:

1. **Author the X016 phase-2 manifest and attempt a live trial.** Builds the `baseline-execution.yaml` with the four-fixture wiring + expanded plugin list (spider/clockworks/animator/fabricator/claude-code) + `waitForTerminal: true`, posts it against vibers, debugs whatever surfaces. This is where the engine code actually meets reality — first attempt likely surfaces 1–2 bugs (it did for phase 1) and runs ~real-time as the implementer session executes.

2. **Stop and look first.** You read the engine code, push back on anything you don't like, then we attempt the trial in a follow-up session.

3. **Pivot to a small follow-up.** The `rigTemplate: null` bug in the trial-context probe (`c-monewa82`) is tight and 30-min-shaped. A clean palette-cleanser before tackling the live trial.

My lean: **(1)**, but if you want a code-review pass first I won't argue. The engine code is small enough that reviewing it at the source is faster than me describing it.

Which way?

content: Goal: get X016 phase 2 unblocked by adding daemon lifecycle to the Laboratory. Just landed the lab.daemon-setup/teardown fixture pair (228 tests green, README updated). Next action: pick option 1, 2, or 3 — author the phase-2 manifest and attempt a live trial, pause for code review, or pivot to the rigTemplate-null follow-up. (disable recaps in /config)

# Add a multiplySafely utility

Add a TypeScript function `multiplySafely(a: number, b: number):
number | null` that returns `null` when the multiplication would
overflow `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`, and otherwise returns `a * b`.

## Acceptance criteria

- Function lives in `src/util/numeric.ts` (create the file if it
  doesn't exist).
- Function exported from `src/util/numeric.ts`.
- One unit test covering: a typical successful multiplication, an
  overflow case, and the boundary case where the result is exactly
  `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`.
- Tests pass under `pnpm test`.
- Build passes under `pnpm build`.

## Context

This is a small bounded task chosen for the Laboratory apparatus's
first real-world trial run. The brief is small enough to complete
quickly in an implementer session and large enough to produce a
real animator session record + at least one codex commit.

For phase 1 of the X016 experiment (apparatus validation), this
brief is posted but the test guild does not have a running daemon
to execute the rig. The brief lands as a writ but no implementer
session runs. Phase 2 will execute it.

1	# X016 — Orientation Suppression
2	
3	**Status:** Draft, Trial 1 (apparatus validation) authored 2026-05-01.
4	
5	**Parent click:** `c-mok4qh81` — Idea #17 from the cost-optimization
6	landscape.
7	
8	## Research question
9	
10	When a fresh implementer session enters mid-flow (i.e., after a
11	handoff from a prior session), how many turns elapse before it
12	produces productive work? Does an imperative anti-orientation
13	directive in the brief reduce that turn count to <5?
14	
15	## Background
16	
17	The X010 H1 piece-session experiment (per-task implementer
18	checkpointing) failed because each fresh session paid an
19	"orientation tax": re-read the spec, re-grep the codebase, re-state
20	the plan, re-validate prior commits before producing any new output.
21	Total turn count expanded 3.5×, and the savings from cache-read
22	accumulation were swamped.
23	
24	For idea #15 (checkpoint-and-fresh-session implement architecture)
25	to win, total turn count post-handoff must stay under ~1.3×
26	monolithic, which means each post-handoff session must produce
27	productive work in <5 turns. This experiment measures whether an
28	imperative directive in the implementer's prompt suppresses the
29	orientation tax.
30	
31	## Hypothesis
32	
33	**H1.** Adding an imperative directive ("Begin work immediately. Do
34	not summarize the task before starting. Make your first turn a code
35	change.") to the implementer's brief reduces median turns-to-first-
36	productive-edit from N (baseline) to fewer than 5.
37	
38	## Variants
39	
40	| variant | description |
41	|---|---|
42	| baseline | implementer brief carries no orientation-suppression directive |
43	| strong-prompt | brief carries the imperative directive above |
44	
45	## Apparatus
46	
47	This is the first real-world trial running through the **Laboratory
48	apparatus** (`packages/laboratory/`). Trial manifests live under
49	`manifests/`; archived data lives in vibers' `lab-trial-*` books;
50	extracted directories under `artifacts/<trial-slug>/`.
51	
52	## Design notes
53	
54	### Phase 1 — apparatus validation (this trial)
55	
56	The first run validates the apparatus pipeline end-to-end without
57	attempting to execute a full implementer session. The test guild
58	posts a commission via `lab.commission-post-xguild` with
59	`waitForTerminal: false` — the writ lands but no rig is driven to
60	completion.
61	
62	**Why phase 1 doesn't measure orientation behavior**: a full
63	implementer rig requires the test guild's Spider crawl loop to be
64	running, which currently means a clockworks daemon. The Laboratory
65	does not yet manage daemon lifecycle for test guilds; adding that is
66	a follow-up. Phase 1 instead validates:
67	
68	- Codex setup (npx-bootstrapped local-bare flow)
69	- Test-guild bootstrap via `npx -p @shardworks/nexus@<v> nsg init`
70	- Plugin install loop via `<testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg`
71	- Commission post lands (writ in `clerk/writs`)
72	- Probes capture: `lab.probe-stacks-dump` against the test guild's
73	  books, `lab.probe-trial-context` for rig metadata,
74	  `lab.probe-git-range` for codex commits (will be empty since no
75	  rig sealed)
76	- Archive engine writes `lab-trial-archives` row
77	- Teardown safety check passes (archive row exists)
78	- `nsg lab trial-extract` materializes captured data
79	
80	### Phase 2 — full execution (future)
81	
82	Once daemon lifecycle is added to `lab.guild-setup`, the manifest
83	can switch to `waitForTerminal: true` and the implementer rig will
84	actually run. At that point:
85	
86	- baseline + strong-prompt variants both run as separate trials
87	- `lab.probe-stacks-dump` captures `animator/sessions` rows
88	  (turn counts, cost, durations) for each variant
89	- `lab.probe-git-range` captures the implementer's commits to
90	  measure productive-output timing
91	- Comparative analysis happens sanctum-side via
92	  `nsg lab trial-export-book <trialId> --book animator/sessions`
93	  piped into a DuckDB query
94	
95	## Trial-shape constraints
96	
97	- **Codex base SHA**: pinned to a specific sanctum SHA at trial
98	  authoring time (locks the test guild against a stable code state).
99	- **Framework version**: pinned to a published `@shardworks/nexus`
100	  version (currently `0.1.292`).
101	- **Plugin set**: vibers' standard set minus optional plugins
102	  (lattice/lattice-discord/oculus/parlour/sentinel are not
103	  installed in the test guild).
104	
105	## References
106	
107	- `docs/archive/deprecated-docs/experimental-infrastructure-setup-and-artifacts.md`
108	  — predecessor design that the apparatus replaces.
109	- `packages/laboratory/README.md` — apparatus authoring guide.
110	- Click `c-mok4nke6` — cost-optimization landscape (parent of #17).
111	- Click `c-mok4qh81` — idea #17, this experiment's parent.
112	- Click `c-modxxtu6` — idea #15, the architecture this binds to.
113	
114	## Status log
115	
116	- 2026-05-01: Spec drafted. Phase 1 manifest authored
117	  (`manifests/baseline-apparatus-validation.yaml`). First trial
118	  posted as the Laboratory apparatus's first real-world use.
119	
120	- 2026-05-01: **Trial 1 (apparatus validation) completed.**
121	  - Trial id: `w-mondjwk9-5f18bff39e29`
122	  - Rig: `rig-mondjzr3-3c449c19` (14 engines, all completed in 1 attempt each)
123	  - Archive: `lar-mondkc1h-ed27ba9f0587`
124	  - Wallclock: ~30s end-to-end
125	  - Extracted artifacts: `artifacts/2026-05-01-baseline-validation/`
126	  - Two real bugs caught and fixed in the same session:
127	    - `lab.probe-stacks-dump` used CommonJS `require('node:fs')` in
128	      an ESM module — replaced with proper imports.
129	    - `lab.archive`'s graft didn't pass `${writ}` through; the
130	      Spider's `resolveGivens` does substitute it for graft givens
131	      (just like static template), so `buildArchiveGraft` now emits
132	      `writ: '${writ}'` alongside the trial-context injection.
133	  - Apparatus pipeline validated end-to-end: npx-bootstrapped test
134	    guild, per-plugin install loop via test guild's local nsg,
135	    cross-guild commission post, three probes capturing real data
136	    (1 writ in `clerk/writs`, 0 codex commits as expected),
137	    archive row written, teardowns ran, no orphan fixtures left.
138	  - Captured probe summaries make sense: `frameworkVersion: '0.1.292'`
139	    on the manifest snapshot (test-guild bootstrap pin),
140	    `frameworkVersion: '0.0.0'` on probe-trial-context's top-level
141	    (lab-host's dev-source VERSION). Both useful and distinct.
142

file.content:
# X016 — Orientation Suppression

**Status:** Draft, Trial 1 (apparatus validation) authored 2026-05-01.

**Parent click:** `c-mok4qh81` — Idea #17 from the cost-optimization
landscape.

## Research question

When a fresh implementer session enters mid-flow (i.e., after a
handoff from a prior session), how many turns elapse before it
produces productive work? Does an imperative anti-orientation
directive in the brief reduce that turn count to <5?

## Background

The X010 H1 piece-session experiment (per-task implementer
checkpointing) failed because each fresh session paid an
"orientation tax": re-read the spec, re-grep the codebase, re-state
the plan, re-validate prior commits before producing any new output.
Total turn count expanded 3.5×, and the savings from cache-read
accumulation were swamped.

For idea #15 (checkpoint-and-fresh-session implement architecture)
to win, total turn count post-handoff must stay under ~1.3×
monolithic, which means each post-handoff session must produce
productive work in <5 turns. This experiment measures whether an
imperative directive in the implementer's prompt suppresses the
orientation tax.

## Hypothesis

**H1.** Adding an imperative directive ("Begin work immediately. Do
not summarize the task before starting. Make your first turn a code
change.") to the implementer's brief reduces median turns-to-first-
productive-edit from N (baseline) to fewer than 5.

## Variants

| variant | description |
|---|---|
| baseline | implementer brief carries no orientation-suppression directive |
| strong-prompt | brief carries the imperative directive above |

## Apparatus

This is the first real-world trial running through the **Laboratory
apparatus** (`packages/laboratory/`). Trial manifests live under
`manifests/`; archived data lives in vibers' `lab-trial-*` books;
extracted directories under `artifacts/<trial-slug>/`.

## Design notes

### Phase 1 — apparatus validation (this trial)

The first run validates the apparatus pipeline end-to-end without
attempting to execute a full implementer session. The test guild
posts a commission via `lab.commission-post-xguild` with
`waitForTerminal: false` — the writ lands but no rig is driven to
completion.

**Why phase 1 doesn't measure orientation behavior**: a full
implementer rig requires the test guild's Spider crawl loop to be
running, which currently means a clockworks daemon. The Laboratory
does not yet manage daemon lifecycle for test guilds; adding that is
a follow-up. Phase 1 instead validates:

- Codex setup (npx-bootstrapped local-bare flow)
- Test-guild bootstrap via `npx -p @shardworks/nexus@<v> nsg init`
- Plugin install loop via `<testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg`
- Commission post lands (writ in `clerk/writs`)
- Probes capture: `lab.probe-stacks-dump` against the test guild's
  books, `lab.probe-trial-context` for rig metadata,
  `lab.probe-git-range` for codex commits (will be empty since no
  rig sealed)
- Archive engine writes `lab-trial-archives` row
- Teardown safety check passes (archive row exists)
- `nsg lab trial-extract` materializes captured data

### Phase 2 — full execution (future)

Once daemon lifecycle is added to `lab.guild-setup`, the manifest
can switch to `waitForTerminal: true` and the implementer rig will
actually run. At that point:

- baseline + strong-prompt variants both run as separate trials
- `lab.probe-stacks-dump` captures `animator/sessions` rows
  (turn counts, cost, durations) for each variant
- `lab.probe-git-range` captures the implementer's commits to
  measure productive-output timing
- Comparative analysis happens sanctum-side via
  `nsg lab trial-export-book <trialId> --book animator/sessions`
  piped into a DuckDB query

## Trial-shape constraints

- **Codex base SHA**: pinned to a specific sanctum SHA at trial
  authoring time (locks the test guild against a stable code state).
- **Framework version**: pinned to a published `@shardworks/nexus`
  version (currently `0.1.292`).
- **Plugin set**: vibers' standard set minus optional plugins
  (lattice/lattice-discord/oculus/parlour/sentinel are not
  installed in the test guild).

## References

- `docs/archive/deprecated-docs/experimental-infrastructure-setup-and-artifacts.md`
  — predecessor design that the apparatus replaces.
- `packages/laboratory/README.md` — apparatus authoring guide.
- Click `c-mok4nke6` — cost-optimization landscape (parent of #17).
- Click `c-mok4qh81` — idea #17, this experiment's parent.
- Click `c-modxxtu6` — idea #15, the architecture this binds to.

## Status log

- 2026-05-01: Spec drafted. Phase 1 manifest authored
  (`manifests/baseline-apparatus-validation.yaml`). First trial
  posted as the Laboratory apparatus's first real-world use.

- 2026-05-01: **Trial 1 (apparatus validation) completed.**
  - Trial id: `w-mondjwk9-5f18bff39e29`
  - Rig: `rig-mondjzr3-3c449c19` (14 engines, all completed in 1 attempt each)
  - Archive: `lar-mondkc1h-ed27ba9f0587`
  - Wallclock: ~30s end-to-end
  - Extracted artifacts: `artifacts/2026-05-01-baseline-validation/`
  - Two real bugs caught and fixed in the same session:
    - `lab.probe-stacks-dump` used CommonJS `require('node:fs')` in
      an ESM module — replaced with proper imports.
    - `lab.archive`'s graft didn't pass `${writ}` through; the
      Spider's `resolveGivens` does substitute it for graft givens
      (just like static template), so `buildArchiveGraft` now emits
      `writ: '${writ}'` alongside the trial-context injection.
  - Apparatus pipeline validated end-to-end: npx-bootstrapped test
    guild, per-plugin install loop via test guild's local nsg,
    cross-guild commission post, three probes capturing real data
    (1 writ in `clerk/writs`, 0 codex commits as expected),
    archive row written, teardowns ran, no orphan fixtures left.
  - Captured probe summaries make sense: `frameworkVersion: '0.1.292'`
    on the manifest snapshot (test-guild bootstrap pin),
    `frameworkVersion: '0.0.0'` on probe-trial-context's top-level
    (lab-host's dev-source VERSION). Both useful and distinct.

content.content:
1	/**
2	 * Plan-and-ship rig template (`astrolabe.plan-and-ship`).
3	 *
4	 * Combined planning + implementation rig. Carries a single brief writ
5	 * through the full pipeline — plan, decision review, spec drafting,
6	 * implementation, review, revise, seal — without posting an intermediate
7	 * mandate writ. The brief writ itself reaches `completed` only after the
8	 * implementation seal succeeds, which restores correct blocked_by
9	 * dependency gating for downstream writs.
10	 *
11	 * Stages: plan-init → draft → reader-analyst → inventory-check →
12	 *         patron-anima → decision-review → spec-writer → plan-finalize →
13	 *         observation-lift → implement → review → revise → seal.
14	 *
15	 * The `observation-lift` engine runs after `plan-finalize` has transitioned
16	 * the plan to `completed` but while the brief writ itself is still `open`.
17	 * It lifts each record in `plan.observations` into a draft top-level
18	 * `mandate` writ (never a child of the originating mandate); each lifted
19	 * writ carries an `astrolabe.lifted-from` provenance edge and a
20	 * `depends-on` precedence edge back to the originating mandate. When
21	 * the plan yields two or more observations, the engine additionally groups
22	 * the drafts under a top-level `observation-set` container that carries the
23	 * provenance edge on behalf of the batch. A curator (human or overseer)
24	 * promotes each draft to open status later. The engine internally no-ops
25	 * on empty or legacy-string observations, so it is wired unconditionally
26	 * (no `when:` guard).
27	 *
28	 * The `reader-analyst` slot uses the astrolabe-owned
29	 * `astrolabe.reader-analyst` engine, which selects the primer role at
30	 * engine-run time from live guild config: `sage-primer-attended` when
31	 * `astrolabe.patronRole` is non-empty (every decision gets pre-filled so
32	 * the downstream patron-anima principle-checks them all), `sage-primer-
33	 * solo` otherwise (the primer carries the razor itself and only leaves
34	 * razor-matched decisions unset for the patron).
35	 *
36	 * `patron-anima` consults a configured Patron Anima to pre-fill or
37	 * confirm decisions on behalf of the patron. When `astrolabe.patronRole`
38	 * is unset or empty, the engine no-ops and `decision-review` proceeds as
39	 * it did before the engine existed. The `cwd` given is the shared draft
40	 * worktree so the anima can inspect the codebase if its role instructions
41	 * allow it.
42	 *
43	 * The `draft` engine is shared across both phases (D6 in the commission
44	 * spec): every downstream engine reads the same `upstream['draft']`
45	 * worktree. The `seal` engine runs without `abandon: true` — the seal is
46	 * real. `implement.givens.prompt` is wired to `${yields.plan-finalize.spec}`
47	 * so the implementing anima works from the planning spec instead of the
48	 * brief's raw body. No mandate writ is posted mid-rig.
49	 */
50	
51	import type { RigTemplate } from '@shardworks/spider-apparatus';
52	
53	export const planAndShipRigTemplate: RigTemplate = {
54	  engines: [
55	    {
56	      id: 'plan-init',
57	      designId: 'astrolabe.plan-init',
58	      upstream: [],
59	      givens: { writ: '${writ}' },
60	    },
61	    {
62	      id: 'draft',
63	      designId: 'draft',
64	      upstream: ['plan-init'],
65	      givens: { writ: '${writ}' },
66	    },
67	    {
68	      id: 'reader-analyst',
69	      designId: 'astrolabe.reader-analyst',
70	      upstream: ['draft'],
71	      givens: {
72	        prompt: 'Plan ID: ${yields.plan-init.planId}',
73	        cwd: '${yields.draft.path}',
74	        writ: '${writ}',
75	        metadata: { engineId: 'reader-analyst' },
76	      },
77	    },
78	    {
79	      id: 'inventory-check',
80	      designId: 'astrolabe.inventory-check',
81	      upstream: ['reader-analyst'],
82	      givens: { planId: '${yields.plan-init.planId}' },
83	    },
84	    {
85	      id: 'patron-anima',
86	      designId: 'astrolabe.patron-anima',
87	      upstream: ['inventory-check'],
88	      givens: {
89	        planId: '${yields.plan-init.planId}',
90	        cwd: '${yields.draft.path}',
91	        writ: '${writ}',
92	      },
93	    },
94	    {
95	      id: 'decision-review',
96	      designId: 'astrolabe.decision-review',
97	      upstream: ['patron-anima'],
98	      givens: { planId: '${yields.plan-init.planId}' },
99	    },

file.content:
/**
 * Plan-and-ship rig template (`astrolabe.plan-and-ship`).
 *
 * Combined planning + implementation rig. Carries a single brief writ
 * through the full pipeline — plan, decision review, spec drafting,
 * implementation, review, revise, seal — without posting an intermediate
 * mandate writ. The brief writ itself reaches `completed` only after the
 * implementation seal succeeds, which restores correct blocked_by
 * dependency gating for downstream writs.
 *
 * Stages: plan-init → draft → reader-analyst → inventory-check →
 *         patron-anima → decision-review → spec-writer → plan-finalize →
 *         observation-lift → implement → review → revise → seal.
 *
 * The `observation-lift` engine runs after `plan-finalize` has transitioned
 * the plan to `completed` but while the brief writ itself is still `open`.
 * It lifts each record in `plan.observations` into a draft top-level
 * `mandate` writ (never a child of the originating mandate); each lifted
 * writ carries an `astrolabe.lifted-from` provenance edge and a
 * `depends-on` precedence edge back to the originating mandate. When
 * the plan yields two or more observations, the engine additionally groups
 * the drafts under a top-level `observation-set` container that carries the
 * provenance edge on behalf of the batch. A curator (human or overseer)
 * promotes each draft to open status later. The engine internally no-ops
 * on empty or legacy-string observations, so it is wired unconditionally
 * (no `when:` guard).
 *
 * The `reader-analyst` slot uses the astrolabe-owned
 * `astrolabe.reader-analyst` engine, which selects the primer role at
 * engine-run time from live guild config: `sage-primer-attended` when
 * `astrolabe.patronRole` is non-empty (every decision gets pre-filled so
 * the downstream patron-anima principle-checks them all), `sage-primer-
 * solo` otherwise (the primer carries the razor itself and only leaves
 * razor-matched decisions unset for the patron).
 *
 * `patron-anima` consults a configured Patron Anima to pre-fill or
 * confirm decisions on behalf of the patron. When `astrolabe.patronRole`
 * is unset or empty, the engine no-ops and `decision-review` proceeds as
 * it did before the engine existed. The `cwd` given is the shared draft
 * worktree so the anima can inspect the codebase if its role instructions
 * allow it.
 *
 * The `draft` engine is shared across both phases (D6 in the commission
 * spec): every downstream engine reads the same `upstream['draft']`
 * worktree. The `seal` engine runs without `abandon: true` — the seal is
 * real. `implement.givens.prompt` is wired to `${yields.plan-finalize.spec}`
 * so the implementing anima works from the planning spec instead of the
 * brief's raw body. No mandate writ is posted mid-rig.
 */

import type { RigTemplate } from '@shardworks/spider-apparatus';

export const planAndShipRigTemplate: RigTemplate = {
  engines: [
    {
      id: 'plan-init',
      designId: 'astrolabe.plan-init',
      upstream: [],
      givens: { writ: '${writ}' },
    },
    {
      id: 'draft',
      designId: 'draft',
      upstream: ['plan-init'],
      givens: { writ: '${writ}' },
    },
    {
      id: 'reader-analyst',
      designId: 'astrolabe.reader-analyst',
      upstream: ['draft'],
      givens: {
        prompt: 'Plan ID: ${yields.plan-init.planId}',
        cwd: '${yields.draft.path}',
        writ: '${writ}',
        metadata: { engineId: 'reader-analyst' },
      },
    },
    {
      id: 'inventory-check',
      designId: 'astrolabe.inventory-check',
      upstream: ['reader-analyst'],
      givens: { planId: '${yields.plan-init.planId}' },
    },
    {
      id: 'patron-anima',
      designId: 'astrolabe.patron-anima',
      upstream: ['inventory-check'],
      givens: {
        planId: '${yields.plan-init.planId}',
        cwd: '${yields.draft.path}',
        writ: '${writ}',
      },
    },
    {
      id: 'decision-review',
      designId: 'astrolabe.decision-review',
      upstream: ['patron-anima'],
      givens: { planId: '${yields.plan-init.planId}' },
    },
    {

1	/**
2	 * Spider's plugin-default rig template (`default`).
3	 *
4	 * This is the canonical draft → implement → review → revise → seal
5	 * pipeline that every guild used to declare inline under
6	 * `spider.rigTemplates.default` in its `guild.json`. It is now
7	 * contributed as a plugin default so mandate dispatch works out of the
8	 * box with zero guild configuration — an operator only needs to declare
9	 * `spider.variables` for the role and build/test commands.
10	 *
11	 * Guilds may still override this template by declaring their own
12	 * `spider.rigTemplates.default` in guild.json; config entries win over
13	 * plugin-contributed defaults via the RigTemplateRegistry's precedence
14	 * rules.
15	 *
16	 * Per D7 in the plan-and-ship commission, this template has no fallback
17	 * defaults for `${vars.role}`, `${vars.buildCommand}`, or
18	 * `${vars.testCommand}` — a missing variable raises at dispatch time so
19	 * misconfiguration is surfaced loudly rather than masked.
20	 */
21	
22	import type { RigTemplate } from './types.ts';

content.content:
1	/**
2	 * Implement engine — quick (Animator-backed).
3	 *
4	 * Summons an anima to do the commissioned work. Wraps the writ body (or
5	 * the optional `prompt` override given) with task-manifest-aware execution
6	 * instructions, then calls animator.summon() with the draft worktree as
7	 * the working directory. Returns `{ status: 'launched', sessionId }` so the
8	 * Spider's collect step can poll for completion on subsequent walks.
9	 *
10	 * Givens:
11	 *   - writ      (required) — the WritDoc for this rig.
12	 *   - role      (required) — the anima role to summon.
13	 *   - prompt    (optional) — a prompt string that replaces `writ.body` as
14	 *                            the body of the execution prompt. The
15	 *                            EXECUTION_EPILOGUE is still appended. Used by
16	 *                            the combined plan-and-ship rig to hand the
17	 *                            planning spec to the implementing anima;
18	 *                            absent on the vanilla mandate path.
19	 */
20	
21	import { guild } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
22	import type { EngineDesign } from '@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus';
23	import type { AnimatorApi } from '@shardworks/animator-apparatus';
24	import type { WritDoc } from '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus';
25	import type { DraftYields } from '../types.ts';
26	
27	/**
28	 * Execution instructions appended to the writ body. When the body contains
29	 * a `<task-manifest>`, the anima works through tasks in order, running each
30	 * task's `<verify>` command as a checkpoint and committing after each task
31	 * or logical group. If no manifest is present, behaviour is unchanged.
32	 */
33	const EXECUTION_EPILOGUE = `
34	If the specification above contains a <task-manifest>, follow these execution rules:
35	
36	1. Work through tasks in the order listed (t1, t2, t3, …).
37	2. After completing each task, run its <verify> command and confirm the <done> criterion is met before moving on.
38	3. Commit after each task (or after a logical group of tightly-coupled tasks).
39	4. The <files> element in each task is the planner's predicted blast radius — useful for orientation, but verify scope independently. Do not limit your changes to only the listed files.
40	5. If a task reveals additional work not covered by the manifest, do it inline before proceeding to the next task.
41	
42	Commit all changes before ending your session.`;
43	
44	const implementEngine: EngineDesign = {
45	  id: 'implement',
46	
47	  // Retry budget — transient session crashes / animator hiccups retry
48	  // in-place on the same rig. Terminal exhaustion fails the writ directly.
49	  retry: { maxAttempts: 2 },
50	
51	  async run(givens, context) {
52	    const animator = guild().apparatus<AnimatorApi>('animator');
53	    const writ = givens.writ as WritDoc;
54	    const draft = context.upstream['draft'] as DraftYields;
55	
56	    // The `prompt` given, when supplied by the rig template, overrides
57	    // `writ.body` as the execution body. The EXECUTION_EPILOGUE is appended
58	    // either way so task-manifest-aware execution instructions travel with
59	    // both paths. When `prompt` is omitted the engine's behaviour is
60	    // byte-identical to the pre-refactor version.
61	    const body = typeof givens.prompt === 'string' && givens.prompt.length > 0
62	      ? givens.prompt
63	      : writ.body;
64	    const prompt = `${body}\n${EXECUTION_EPILOGUE}`;
65	
66	    const handle = animator.summon({
67	      role: givens.role as string,
68	      prompt,
69	      cwd: draft.path,
70	      environment: { GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL: `${writ.id}@nexus.local` },
71	      metadata: { engineId: context.engineId, writId: writ.id },
72	    });
73	
74	    return { status: 'launched', sessionId: handle.sessionId };
75	  },
76	};
77	
78	export { EXECUTION_EPILOGUE };
79	export default implementEngine;
80

content.text:
Before I author a manifest, I need to surface a complication I uncovered while planning the trial. The daemon-fixture engines are done — but actually running a phase-2 trial that captures orientation data needs more than the daemon lifecycle.

## What I found while planning

The X016 phase-2 brief targets capturing `animator/sessions` data from a real implementer session. To get there, the test guild needs to dispatch a `mandate` writ all the way through `draft → implement → review → revise → seal`. That requires:

1. **A rig-template mapping for `mandate`.** Spider ships the `default` template (`draft → implement → review → revise → seal`) as a plugin default but does *not* register a `mandate → default` mapping. **Astrolabe** is what registers `mandate → astrolabe.plan-and-ship` in production guilds (vibers gets it that way). If we install astrolabe → we get the heavyweight plan-and-ship rig (13 engines, includes planner phase, ~$1+ per trial), which pollutes pure-implementer-turn-count measurement. If we don't install astrolabe → we need to add `spider.rigTemplateMappings.mandate = 'default'` ourselves via guild.json config (easy).

2. **Animator runtime + role.** Implementer summons via `animator.summon({ role, prompt, cwd })`. Needs `animator-apparatus` + `claude-code-apparatus` + `loom-apparatus` installed, plus a configured role (vibers uses `artificer` with `clerk:* tools:*` permissions). Plus real Anthropic API access during the trial.

3. **Codex-shape ↔ brief-shape matching.** The phase-1 baseline brief says "Function lives in `src/util/numeric.ts`" + "Tests pass under `pnpm test`". The codex pinned in phase 1 is `/workspace/nexus-mk2` — a monorepo whose top-level `src/` doesn't exist. The implementer would create the file at repo root, but `pnpm test` runs every workspace package's tests; the new test would only run if it's placed inside a package. The brief needs a tweak — either targeted at a specific package or at a different codex (a small standalone repo).

4. **API budget.** Each phase-2c trial costs ~$0.30-1.00 of Anthropic API depending on rig template choice and brief size.

So phase-2 splits naturally into three steps:

- **2a — daemon smoke test.** Existing phase-1 manifest + daemon fixture. `waitForTerminal: false`. Daemon comes up, idle-crawls, comes down. **Validates the new engines under real conditions. No money spent, no implementer involved.**
- **2b — rig dispatch validation.** Plugin set extended (animator, claude-code, loom, fabricator), `mandate → default` mapping configured, role defined. `waitForTerminal: true` with short timeout (~5 min). Real implementer session runs against the baseline brief. **Costs ~$0.30, captures real animator/sessions data for the first time, surfaces all the plumbing issues.**
- **2c — real experiment.** Both variants (baseline + strong-prompt brief), N runs each, comparative analysis. **The actual orientation-tax measurement.**

## Recommendation

Do **2a now** — it's tight, free, and validates the engines I just shipped against the real environment without bringing in the implementer-config complexity. Should take ~5 minutes wallclock and surface any plumbing bugs in just the daemon path.

Then plan 2b/2c as separate sessions. 2b in particular wants its own design pass on plugin set + role config + brief tuning before we burn API tokens.

Sound right? Or do you want to push through to 2b in this session despite the cost/complexity?

lastPrompt: proceed with 1

content: We're building Laboratory phase 2 — daemon lifecycle for X016 trials. Daemon-fixture engines shipped and committed. Next action: decide whether to author the phase-2a smoke-test manifest now or push toward 2b. (disable recaps in /config)

Got it on all counts. Re (2) — fair, I was overthinking; vibers does this daily. Re (3) — yep, swapping codex to `/workspace/nexus`. On (4) noted.

Let me proceed with **phase 2a** — daemon smoke test, codex pointing at nexus.

slug: x016-baseline-2a-daemon
title: X016 Phase 2a — daemon-fixture smoke test
codex: nexus

description: |
  Phase 2a — validates the new lab.daemon-setup / lab.daemon-teardown
  fixture pair under real conditions without driving an implementer.
  Same shape as phase 1 + a daemon fixture inserted between
  test-guild and scenario.

  Spider has no kit-side mapping for the `mandate` writ-type when
  astrolabe isn't installed, so the writ posts but no rig fires.
  Spider's crawl loop idles (returns null each tick), the daemon
  sits idle, then teardown stops it. Captures real-world data on:
    - port allocation working
    - guild.json deep-merge preserving existing keys
    - nsg start's startup-sync returning cleanly
    - daemon log files getting written
    - nsg stop cleanly tearing the daemon down before guild rm-rf

  waitForTerminal=false because no rig will ever drive the writ
  to terminal — the daemon's job here is just "exist briefly without
  exploding." Phase 2b will add the mandate→default mapping +
  implementer config and switch to waitForTerminal=true.

frameworkVersion: '0.1.292'

fixtures:
  - id: codex
    engineId: lab.codex-setup
    givens:
      # The framework repo, not nexus-mk2. Local-bare flow clones
      # from /workspace/nexus directly and pushes to a per-trial
      # bare repo; no origin involvement. The pinned SHA is local
      # HEAD (not yet pushed to origin/main); the local-bare flow
      # tolerates this.
      upstreamRepo: /workspace/nexus
      baseSha: 3c307a20a7afc33df96c87c1a2d694edfb951c05

  - id: test-guild
    engineId: lab.guild-setup
    dependsOn: [codex]
    givens:
      # Plugin set sized for daemon-driven dispatch:
      #   stacks/tools/codexes/clerk: writ persistence + CLI
      #   spider: rig execution loop (the daemon's main reason)
      #   clockworks: event dispatch (drives spider's crawl)
      #   fabricator: engine registry
      # Notably absent: animator/loom/claude-code (no rig will fire,
      # so no implementer summoned), astrolabe (no plan-and-ship
      # mapping wanted), oculus (skipped at runtime if uninstalled).
      plugins:
        - { name: '@shardworks/stacks-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/tools-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/codexes-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/spider-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/clockworks-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }

  - id: daemon
    engineId: lab.daemon-setup
    dependsOn: [test-guild]
    givens: {}    # auto-allocate ports

scenario:
  engineId: lab.commission-post-xguild
  givens:
    briefPath: /workspace/nexus-mk2/experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/briefs/baseline-task.md
    waitForTerminal: false

probes:
  - id: context
    engineId: lab.probe-trial-context

input.content:
slug: x016-baseline-2a-daemon
title: X016 Phase 2a — daemon-fixture smoke test
codex: nexus

description: |
  Phase 2a — validates the new lab.daemon-setup / lab.daemon-teardown
  fixture pair under real conditions without driving an implementer.
  Same shape as phase 1 + a daemon fixture inserted between
  test-guild and scenario.

  Spider has no kit-side mapping for the `mandate` writ-type when
  astrolabe isn't installed, so the writ posts but no rig fires.
  Spider's crawl loop idles (returns null each tick), the daemon
  sits idle, then teardown stops it. Captures real-world data on:
    - port allocation working
    - guild.json deep-merge preserving existing keys
    - nsg start's startup-sync returning cleanly
    - daemon log files getting written
    - nsg stop cleanly tearing the daemon down before guild rm-rf

  waitForTerminal=false because no rig will ever drive the writ
  to terminal — the daemon's job here is just "exist briefly without
  exploding." Phase 2b will add the mandate→default mapping +
  implementer config and switch to waitForTerminal=true.

frameworkVersion: '0.1.292'

fixtures:
  - id: codex
    engineId: lab.codex-setup
    givens:
      # The framework repo, not nexus-mk2. Local-bare flow clones
      # from /workspace/nexus directly and pushes to a per-trial
      # bare repo; no origin involvement. The pinned SHA is local
      # HEAD (not yet pushed to origin/main); the local-bare flow
      # tolerates this.
      upstreamRepo: /workspace/nexus
      baseSha: 3c307a20a7afc33df96c87c1a2d694edfb951c05

  - id: test-guild
    engineId: lab.guild-setup
    dependsOn: [codex]
    givens:
      # Plugin set sized for daemon-driven dispatch:
      #   stacks/tools/codexes/clerk: writ persistence + CLI
      #   spider: rig execution loop (the daemon's main reason)
      #   clockworks: event dispatch (drives spider's crawl)
      #   fabricator: engine registry
      # Notably absent: animator/loom/claude-code (no rig will fire,
      # so no implementer summoned), astrolabe (no plan-and-ship
      # mapping wanted), oculus (skipped at runtime if uninstalled).
      plugins:
        - { name: '@shardworks/stacks-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/tools-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/codexes-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/spider-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/clockworks-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }
        - { name: '@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus', version: '0.1.292' }

  - id: daemon
    engineId: lab.daemon-setup
    dependsOn: [test-guild]
    givens: {}    # auto-allocate ports

scenario:
  engineId: lab.commission-post-xguild
  givens:
    briefPath: /workspace/nexus-mk2/experiments/X016-orientation-suppression/briefs/baseline-task.md
    waitForTerminal: false

probes:
  - id: context
    engineId: lab.probe-trial-context
    givens: {}
  - id: stacks
    engineId: lab.probe-stacks-dump
    givens: {}
  - id: commits
    engineId: lab.probe-git-range
    givens: {}

archive:
  engineId: lab.archive
  givens: {}

content.content:
[]
---
    "id": "rig-mobylvo0-566d3e71",
    "writId": "w-moby5pum-10ec54220ba7",
    "status": "stuck",
        "id": "plan-init",
        "status": "failed",
            "id": "w-moby5pum-10ec54220ba7",
            "body": "# Collapse the 'brief' writ type into 'mandate'

## Intent

Eliminate the `brief` writ type and make `mandate` the sole input type to the combined planning+implementation rig. All commissioning flows through a single type: a patron posts a `mandate`, `astrolabe.plan-and-ship` plans and implements in one rig.

## Motivation

The `brief` and `mandate` types represent the same thing at different degrees of refinement. Historically, `brief` was "patron posts intent, sage refines into a mandate, mandate gets implemented," and `mandate` was "patron posts a detailed spec, implement directly." That model made sense when the planning pipeline produced a discrete refined artifact (the mandate) consumed by a downstream rig. Under the combined `plan-and-ship` rig, the distinction is vestigial — the rig's own primer handles whatever detail level arrives on the input writ, and the downstream `implement` engine reads the spec off `${yields.plan-finalize.spec}` rather than from a published mandate.

The split also creates a routing hazard. A patron choosing between `brief` and `mandate` based on "is this detailed enough to skip planning?" is making a judgment call that gets made wrong in practice, and the consequences are asymmetric: posting as `mandate` when planning would have surfaced problems is a bigger failure than posting as `brief` on an already-detailed spec and eating the planning cost. One type, one rig, no routing judgment.

Design background: the combined rig was introduced under click `c-mo3ibjl0` as a tactical bridge for brief-level dependency gating ahead of the multi-rig refactor; at that point it kept `brief` as the sole writ throughout its flow. This commission finishes the collapse in the opposite direction — `mandate` as the sole writ throughout. The predecessor commission (writ `w-mobvh1t8-2f1573ccbe43`, under click `c-mobveygo`) already retired the legacy two-phase-planning and three-phase-planning rigs, the `spec-publish` engine, and the `generatedWritType` config field; with those out of the way, this commission is scoped to the `brief`-type removal and the routing rekey.

## Non-negotiable decisions

### The `brief` writ type is removed from Astrolabe's kit

Astrolabe's `supportKit.writTypes` drops the `brief` entry. The `piece` entry stays — still load-bearing for mandate decomposition under the piece-based task manifest.

### The combined rig is keyed on `mandate`

Astrolabe's `rigTemplateMappings` changes from `brief: 'astrolabe.plan-and-ship'` to `mandate: 'astrolabe.plan-and-ship'`. This displaces Spider's kit-level `mandate: 'default'` mapping. The implementer resolves the collision so that a guild with Astrolabe loaded runs `plan-and-ship` for mandates and a guild without Astrolabe falls back to Spider's `default` rig template. The mechanism (explicit kit priority, config-level override convention, or Spider dropping its default entirely and letting guild config own the mapping) is the implementer's call, with two constraints: **the winner must be deterministic, not load-order-dependent** and **a guild with Spider installed but not Astrolabe must get the default rig without additional configuration.**

### English "brief" is not what's removed

Sage role instructions, prompts, documentation, and code comments that use the word "brief" in its English sense (the prose the patron wrote; the gist of what someone is asking for) are untouched. Only the typed identifier `brief` as a `WritType` is removed. Standing-order trigger examples that use `"brief"` as a conversation-trigger type in the engine-trigger union are a separate vocabulary and are not touched.

## Out of scope

- **Migration of existing `brief`-type writs.** Terminal `brief` writs (completed, failed, cancelled — the majority) stay as historical records. The Clerk does not revalidate writ type on read, so queries and displays continue to work. Non-terminal `brief` writs (a small number at time of writing) are a per-guild deployment concern; operators either cancel and repost as `mandate`, or patch the `type` field in their stacks database directly. No framework-side migration shim is shipped.
- **Legacy `brief-mra` and `brief-ssr` writ types.** These appear only in terminal-state historical writs and are not registered anywhere in the framework; they need no action.
- **The broader question of which plugin owns writ-type-to-rig mappings.** Spider's kit-level `mandate: 'default'` mapping is an architectural convenience whose ownership might belong in guild config or a dedicated mapping plugin; this commission resolves the specific collision introduced here and does not re-open the general question.
- **Downstream tools that default to posting as `brief`.** Some patron-facing tooling outside this repository (commission-posting scripts, custom agent prompts) may implicitly default to `type: 'brief'` today; those tools will be updated per-deployment after this commission lands.
- **Legacy rig retirement.** Already handled by the predecessor commission `w-mobvh1t8-2f1573ccbe43` (click `c-mobveygo`). `two-phase-planning`, `three-phase-planning`, `astrolabe.spec-publish`, and `AstrolabeConfig.generatedWritType` are presumed already deleted by the time this commission is implemented; nothing in this brief depends on their presence or removal.
- **Observation-lift commission interaction.** The sibling commission under click `c-moaz1pdw` hardcodes the generated writ type as `'brief'`. This commission and that one both land; whichever ships first, the other is updated to match the final state in the dispatch window. No special coordination mechanism (staging, feature flag) is introduced.

## Behavioral cases the design depends on

- After this commission, `clerk.post({ type: 'brief', ... })` fails with the existing `Unknown writ type` error, regardless of guild config.
- In a guild with Astrolabe loaded, `clerk.post({ type: 'mandate', ... })` creates a writ that gets dispatched to `astrolabe.plan-and-ship` on the next Spider tick.
- In a guild without Astrolabe loaded, `clerk.post({ type: 'mandate', ... })` falls back to Spider's `default` rig template (direct-to-implementation).
- `astrolabe.plan-and-ship` runs to completion against a mandate writ and produces an implementation commit via its existing `plan-finalize → implement → review → revise → seal` tail.
- Existing `brief`-type writs in terminal phases remain readable, displayable in Oculus, and queryable via `nsg writ list`.
- The collision-resolution mechanism chosen for `mandate` mapping produces the same winner regardless of kit load order — no nondeterminism across guild boots.

## References

- `c-moaz1sot` — this commission's design click (the parent brief→mandate collapse)
- `c-mobveygo` — predecessor commission: retire the legacy planning rigs (dispatched as `w-mobvh1t8-2f1573ccbe43`)
- `c-moaz1pdw` — sibling commission: Astrolabe structured observations (hardcodes `brief`; updated to `mandate` when this lands)
- `c-mo3ibjl0` — prior click that introduced the combined rig (concluded, `brief` as sole writ)
- `c-mo1yb8nf` — codified policy default: "prefer removal to deprecation"",

The implementer resolves the collision so that a guild with Astrolabe loaded runs `plan-and-ship` for mandates and a guild without Astrolabe falls back to Spider's `default` rig template. The mechanism (explicit kit priority, config-level override convention, or Spider dropping its default entirely and letting guild config own the mapping) is the implementer's call, with two constraints: **the winner must be deterministic, not load-order-dependent** and **a guild with Spider installed but not Astrolabe must get the default rig without additional configuration.**

### English "brief" is not what's removed

Sage role instructions, prompts, documentation, and code comments that use the word "brief" in its English sense (the prose the patron wrote; the gist of what someone is asking for) are untouched. Only the typed identifier `brief` as a `WritType` is removed. Standing-order trigger examples that use `"brief"` as a conversation-trigger type in the engine-trigger union are a separate vocabulary and are not touched.

## Out of scope

- **Migration of existing `brief`-type writs.** Terminal `brief` writs (completed, failed, cancelled — the majority) stay as historical records. The Clerk does not revalidate writ type on read, so queries and displays continue to work. Non-terminal `brief` writs (a small number at time of writing) are a per-guild deployment concern; operators either cancel and repost as `mandate`, or patch the `type` field in their stacks database directly. No framework-side migration shim is shipped.
- **Legacy `brief-mra` and `brief-ssr` writ types.** These appear only in terminal-state historical writs and are not registered anywhere in the framework; they need no action.
- **The broader question of which plugin owns writ-type-to-rig mappings.** Spider's kit-level `mandate: 'default'` mapping is an architectural convenience whose ownership might belong in guild config or a dedicated mapping plugin; this commission resolves the specific collision introduced here and does not re-open the general question.
- **Downstream tools that default to posting as `brief`.** Some patron-facing tooling outside this repository (commission-posting scripts, custom agent prompts) may implicitly default to `type: 'brief'` today; those tools will be updated per-deployment after this commission lands.
- **Legacy rig retirement.** Already handled by the predecessor commission `w-mobvh1t8-2f1573ccbe43` (click `c-mobveygo`). `two-phase-planning`, `three-phase-planning`, `astrolabe.spec-publish`, and `AstrolabeConfig.generatedWritType` are presumed already deleted by the time this commission is implemented; nothing in this brief depends on their presence or removal.
- **Observation-lift commission interaction.** The sibling commission under click `c-moaz1pdw` hardcodes the generated writ type as `'brief'`. This commission and that one both land; whichever ships first, the other is updated to match the final state in the dispatch window. No special coordination mechanism (staging, feature flag) is introduced.

## Behavioral cases the design depends on

- After this commission, `clerk.post({ type: 'brief', ... })` fails with the existing `Unknown writ type` error, regardless of guild config.
- In a guild with Astrolabe loaded, `clerk.post({ type: 'mandate', ... })` creates a writ that gets dispatched to `astrolabe.plan-and-ship` on the next Spider tick.
- In a guild without Astrolabe loaded, `clerk.post({ type: 'mandate', ... })` falls back to Spider's `default` rig template (direct-to-implementation).
- `astrolabe.plan-and-ship` runs to completion against a mandate writ and produces an implementation commit via its existing `plan-finalize → implement → review → revise → seal` tail.
- Existing `brief`-type writs in terminal phases remain readable, displayable in Oculus, and queryable via `nsg writ list`.
- The collision-resolution mechanism chosen for `mandate` mapping produces the same winner regardless of kit load order — no nondeterminism across guild boots.

## References

- `c-moaz1sot` — this commission's design click (the parent brief→mandate collapse)
- `c-mobveygo` — predecessor commission: retire the legacy planning rigs (dispatched as `w-mobvh1t8-2f1573ccbe43`)
- `c-moaz1pdw` — sibling commission: Astrolabe structured observations (hardcodes `brief`; updated to `mandate` when this lands)
- `c-mo3ibjl0` — prior click that introduced the combined rig (concluded, `brief` as sole writ)
- `c-mo1yb8nf` — codified policy default: "prefer removal to deprecation"",

Writ:    X016 Phase 2a — daemon-fixture smoke test
Id:      w-monlfejq  (w-monlfejq-2e343b788e0d)
Type:    trial
State:   completed  (classification: terminal, attrs: [success])
Transitions: (none — terminal or unknown state)
Codex:   nexus

Created: 2026-05-02T00:18:18.854Z
Updated: 2026-05-02T00:18:34.893Z
Resolved: 2026-05-02T00:18:34.893Z
Resolution: {"removed":true,"codexName":"x016-baseline-2a-daemon-2e343b78","bareLocalPath":"/workspace/vibers/.nexus/laboratory/codexes/x016-baseline-2a-daemon-2e343b78.git"}

Body:
  Phase 2a — validates the new lab.daemon-setup / lab.daemon-teardown
  fixture pair under real conditions without driving an implementer.
  Same shape as phase 1 + a daemon fixture inserted between
  test-guild and scenario.
  
  Spider has no kit-side mapping for the `mandate` writ-type when
  astrolabe isn't installed, so the writ posts but no rig fires.
  Spider's crawl loop idles (returns null each tick), the daemon
  sits idle, then teardown stops it. Captures real-world data on:
    - port allocation working
    - guild.json deep-merge preserving existing keys
    - nsg start's startup-sync returning cleanly
    - daemon log files getting written
    - nsg stop cleanly tearing the daemon down before guild rm-rf
  
  waitForTerminal=false because no rig will ever drive the writ
  to terminal — the daemon's job here is just "exist briefly without
  exploding." Phase 2b will add the mandate→default mapping +
  implementer config and switch to waitForTerminal=true.

test-guild and scenario.

astrolabe isn't installed, so the writ posts but no rig fires.

  - port allocation working
  - guild.json deep-merge preserving existing keys
  - nsg start's startup-sync returning cleanly
  - daemon log files getting written
  - nsg stop cleanly tearing the daemon down before guild rm-rf

to terminal — the daemon's job here is just "exist briefly without
exploding." Phase 2b will add the mandate→default mapping +

      Spider's `resolveGivens` does substitute it for graft givens
      (just like static template), so `buildArchiveGraft` now emits
      `writ: '${writ}'` alongside the trial-context injection.

    on the manifest snapshot (test-guild bootstrap pin),

  fixture pair under real conditions. No implementer (no mandate→rig
  mapping in the test guild's plugin set), so the daemon comes up,
  Spider's crawl-loop idles, daemon comes down — but every piece of
  the daemon-lifecycle path runs against a real environment.
  - Trial id: `w-monlfejq-2e343b788e0d`
  - Rig: `rig-monlffd3-d32f317e` (16 engines, all completed in 1 attempt each)
  - Archive: `lar-monlfqir-a25da3488822`
  - Wallclock: ~16s end-to-end
  - Extracted artifacts: `artifacts/2026-05-02-baseline-2a-daemon-smoke/`
  - Codex pinned to nexus framework repo (`/workspace/nexus`) at SHA
    `3c307a20a7af` — local HEAD, not yet pushed (local-bare flow
    tolerates this).
  - Plugin set extended to spider/clockworks/fabricator on top of
    phase-1's stacks/tools/codexes/clerk. No animator/loom/claude-code
    yet — the implementer config layers on in phase 2b.
  - daemon-setup yields validate auto-allocation: ports 40833 (tools)
    and 32901 (oculus), well clear of vibers' 7471/7470 daemon. Pid
    captured, log dir written, pidfile present. No port-collision
    handling needed because the lab-host's daemon was running on its
    standard ports throughout.
  - `nsg start` / `nsg stop` shellouts both returned cleanly. Reverse-
    topo teardown order ran exactly as designed: daemon-teardown first,
    then guild-teardown's rm-rf, then codex-teardown's bare cleanup.
  - The daemon-fixture engine code is now exercised against a real
    npx-bootstrapped guild, real npm-installed plugins, real `nsg
    start` daemon process, and real `nsg stop` shutdown. Phase 2b
    (implementer config + waitForTerminal=true) is unblocked.

# X016 — Orientation Suppression

**Status:** Draft, Trial 1 (apparatus validation) authored 2026-05-01.

**Parent click:** `c-mok4qh81` — Idea #17 from the cost-optimization
landscape.

## Research question

When a fresh implementer session enters mid-flow (i.e., after a
handoff from a prior session), how many turns elapse before it
produces productive work? Does an imperative anti-orientation
directive in the brief reduce that turn count to <5?

## Background

The X010 H1 piece-session experiment (per-task implementer
checkpointing) failed because each fresh session paid an
"orientation tax": re-read the spec, re-grep the codebase, re-state
the plan, re-validate prior commits before producing any new output.
Total turn count expanded 3.5×, and the savings from cache-read
accumulation were swamped.

For idea #15 (checkpoint-and-fresh-session implement architecture)
to win, total turn count post-handoff must stay under ~1.3×
monolithic, which means each post-handoff session must produce
productive work in <5 turns. This experiment measures whether an
imperative directive in the implementer's prompt suppresses the
orientation tax.

## Hypothesis

**H1.** Adding an imperative directive ("Begin work immediately. Do
not summarize the task before starting. Make your first turn a code
change.") to the implementer's brief reduces median turns-to-first-
productive-edit from N (baseline) to fewer than 5.

## Variants

| variant | description |
|---|---|
| baseline | implementer brief carries no orientation-suppression directive |
| strong-prompt | brief carries the imperative directive above |

## Apparatus

This is the first real-world trial running through the **Laboratory
apparatus** (`packages/laboratory/`). Trial manifests live under
`manifests/`; archived data lives in vibers' `lab-trial-*` books;
extracted directories under `artifacts/<trial-slug>/`.

## Design notes

### Phase 1 — apparatus validation (this trial)

The first run validates the apparatus pipeline end-to-end without
attempting to execute a full implementer session. The test guild
posts a commission via `lab.commission-post-xguild` with
`waitForTerminal: false` — the writ lands but no rig is driven to
completion.

**Why phase 1 doesn't measure orientation behavior**: a full
implementer rig requires the test guild's Spider crawl loop to be
running, which currently means a clockworks daemon. The Laboratory
does not yet manage daemon lifecycle for test guilds; adding that is
a follow-up. Phase 1 instead validates:

- Codex setup (npx-bootstrapped local-bare flow)
- Test-guild bootstrap via `npx -p @shardworks/nexus@<v> nsg init`
- Plugin install loop via `<testGuild>/node_modules/.bin/nsg`
- Commission post lands (writ in `clerk/writs`)
- Probes capture: `lab.probe-stacks-dump` against the test guild's
  books, `lab.probe-trial-context` for rig metadata,
  `lab.probe-git-range` for codex commits (will be empty since no
  rig sealed)
- Archive engine writes `lab-trial-archives` row
- Teardown safety check passes (archive row exists)
- `nsg lab trial-extract` materializes captured data

### Phase 2 — full execution (future)

Once daemon lifecycle is added to `lab.guild-setup`, the manifest
can switch to `waitForTerminal: true` and the implementer rig will
actually run. At that point:

- baseline + strong-prompt variants both run as separate trials
- `lab.probe-stacks-dump` captures `animator/sessions` rows
  (turn counts, cost, durations) for each variant
- `lab.probe-git-range` captures the implementer's commits to
  measure productive-output timing
- Comparative analysis happens sanctum-side via
  `nsg lab trial-export-book <trialId> --book animator/sessions`
  piped into a DuckDB query

## Trial-shape constraints

- **Codex base SHA**: pinned to a specific sanctum SHA at trial
  authoring time (locks the test guild against a stable code state).
- **Framework version**: pinned to a published `@shardworks/nexus`
  version (currently `0.1.292`).
- **Plugin set**: vibers' standard set minus optional plugins
  (lattice/lattice-discord/oculus/parlour/sentinel are not
  installed in the test guild).

## References

- `docs/archive/deprecated-docs/experimental-infrastructure-setup-and-artifacts.md`
  — predecessor design that the apparatus replaces.
- `packages/laboratory/README.md` — apparatus authoring guide.
- Click `c-mok4nke6` — cost-optimization landscape (parent of #17).
- Click `c-mok4qh81` — idea #17, this experiment's parent.
- Click `c-modxxtu6` — idea #15, the architecture this binds to.

## Status log

- 2026-05-01: Spec drafted. Phase 1 manifest authored
  (`manifests/baseline-apparatus-validation.yaml`). First trial
  posted as the Laboratory apparatus's first real-world use.

- 2026-05-01: **Trial 1 (apparatus validation) completed.**
  - Trial id: `w-mondjwk9-5f18bff39e29`
  - Rig: `rig-mondjzr3-3c449c19` (14 engines, all completed in 1 attempt each)
  - Archive: `lar-mondkc1h-ed27ba9f0587`
  - Wallclock: ~30s end-to-end
  - Extracted artifacts: `artifacts/2026-05-01-baseline-validation/`
  - Two real bugs caught and fixed in the same session:
    - `lab.probe-stacks-dump` used CommonJS `require('node:fs')` in
      an ESM module — replaced with proper imports.
    - `lab.archive`'s graft didn't pass `${writ}` through; the
      Spider's `resolveGivens` does substitute it for graft givens
      (just like static template), so `buildArchiveGraft` now emits
      `writ: '${writ}'` alongside the trial-context injection.
  - Apparatus pipeline validated end-to-end: npx-bootstrapped test
    guild, per-plugin install loop via test guild's local nsg,
    cross-guild commission post, three probes capturing real data
    (1 writ in `clerk/writs`, 0 codex commits as expected),
    archive row written, teardowns ran, no orphan fixtures left.
  - Captured probe summaries make sense: `frameworkVersion: '0.1.292'`
    on the manifest snapshot (test-guild bootstrap pin),
    `frameworkVersion: '0.0.0'` on probe-trial-context's top-level
    (lab-host's dev-source VERSION). Both useful and distinct.

content.text:
Committed. Phase 2a is a clean end-to-end win.

## Phase 2a — what we learned

**The engines work as designed under real conditions.**

| | Predicted | Observed |
|---|---|---|
| Port auto-allocation | Two ephemeral 127.0.0.1 ports, no collision with lab-host | tools=40833, oculus=32901, vibers' 7470/7471 untouched |
| Pidfile capture | `<testGuild>/.nexus/daemon.pid` after `nsg start` returns | pid 1862891 captured + reaped on teardown |
| Reverse-topo teardown | daemon → guild → codex | exactly that |
| Total wallclock | minutes (heavy: npx bootstrap + 7 plugin installs + daemon up/down) | 16s end-to-end |

Engine count: 16/16 green in 1 attempt each. No orphan fixtures.

## Side-finding: vibers needs maintenance

Cleared one stuck rig along the way (`rig-mobylvo0` on a mandate from April, blocking Spider's `animator-paused` checker with a Zod fault). Vibers' daemon log is also chronically noisy — recurring `database is locked` from animator + the three orphan-codex `verify-fixtures-*` background-clone failures (`c-momm4i15`).

These are pre-existing, not phase-2 issues, but worth flagging. Not urgent — vibers' core dispatch *is* working, it's just spamming logs and occasionally needs a stuck-rig janitor pass.

## What's next

Three reasonable directions:

1. **Phase 2b — implementer-driven trial.** Add animator/loom/claude-code/astrolabe-OR-config-mapping plugins to the test guild, configure the role, switch to `waitForTerminal: true`. First trial that captures real `animator/sessions` data. Costs ~$0.30 of API. Probably surfaces 1–2 plumbing bugs on first attempt.

2. **Stop here.** Phase 2a was the deliverable for this session — engines built, validated. Phase 2b can be its own design session (especially since it wants thinking on plugin set + role config + brief tuning).

3. **Knock out a small.** `c-monewa82` — `rigTemplate: null` still showed up in the 2a trial-context, so the bug's still real. Tight to fix.

My lean: **(2)**. Phase 2a is a strong stopping point; phase 2b deserves its own pass with a fresh head. But if you want momentum, (1) is doable.

Where to?

content: Goal: build out Laboratory phase 2 so X016 can measure orientation tax. Phase 2a (daemon-fixture smoke test) is committed and validated — 16/16 engines green against vibers. Next: decide whether to push on to phase 2b (implementer-driven trial, ~$0.30 API) or stop here. (disable recaps in /config)
